task: I need to send a brief reply to a company about a notice I received, and I want to make sure I don’t include anything unnecessary. Can you help me shape it so it covers the point without opening the door to extra back-and-forth?

prefs.explanation: She is unlikely to appreciate hedging or soft-pedaling when she asks for help. Her life pattern is to cut through confusion quickly and verify what matters, so direct answers fit her style. Overly delicate wording would probably feel evasive or wasteful to her.

prefs.short: Prefers concise answers over long explanations
prefs.explanation: She tends to act on practical information and would likely prefer the core point first. Long-winded responses would feel like clutter unless she specifically asks for detail. She seems to value efficiency in communication the same way she values efficiency in paperwork and routines.

prefs.short: Likes organized checklists and clear steps
prefs.explanation: She responds best to information that is easy to verify and follow in order. A structured format would match her record-keeping habits and reduce the chance of missing something important. Loose, rambling prose would be less useful to her than a clean list or step-by-step outline.

She is not looking for warmth that feels performative or sentimental. A steady, neutral tone would likely feel more trustworthy because it avoids trying to manipulate her mood. She probably dislikes being treated as fragile or emotionally steered.

She is very protective of her personal space and information, so she would likely want interactions that do not pry. She would probably be uncomfortable with unnecessary requests for private details or casual data-sharing. A restrained approach that asks only for what is needed would fit her well.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — I can help you keep it brief, neutral, and non-committal.

A good approach is:
- acknowledge receipt,
- state only the minimum fact you need,
- avoid explaining, apologizing, or asking questions,
- don’t add details that invite follow-up.

A simple template:

> Dear [Company Name],  
> I acknowledge receipt of your notice dated [date].  
> Please note that I am reviewing the matter.  
> Regards,  
> [Your Name]

If you want it even shorter:

> Dear [Company Name],  
> I acknowledge receipt of your notice.  
> Regards,  
> [Your Name]

If you paste the notice or tell me the situation in general terms, I can help you draft a version that stays brief and carefully worded.

per_pref.reason: Very direct and no-nonsense: "keep it brief, neutral, and non-committal" and "avoid explaining, apologizing, or asking questions."
Concise and efficient, with a short checklist and two short templates. It explicitly says "If you want it even shorter" and keeps the answer tight.
Well organized with clear steps: "A good approach is:" followed by bullet points, then concrete templates.
Matter-of-fact and neutral throughout, e.g. "brief, neutral, and non-committal" and no sentimental or warm framing.
Strongly minimizes disclosure: "state only the minimum fact you need," "don’t add details that invite follow-up," and the template avoids unnecessary personal information.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that is tighter and less likely to invite a response.

A good minimal version is:

> Dear [Company Name],  
> I acknowledge receipt of your notice dated [date].  
> Regards,  
> [Your Name]

If you want to be even more neutral, you can omit “dated [date]” unless the date is important for identification:

> Dear [Company Name],  
> I acknowledge receipt of your notice.  
> Regards,  
> [Your Name]

If you want, I can also help you make it sound a little more formal or more firm without adding substance.

per_pref.reason: Blunt and direct: "Yes — that is tighter and less likely to invite a response." Then gives the minimal wording without hedging.
Very concise. It provides the core point first and keeps the explanation short, with only two brief template options.
Well organized with clear steps/examples: "A good minimal version is:" followed by a quoted template, then an even shorter alternative.
Matter-of-fact and neutral throughout. Phrases like "acknowledge receipt" and "more formal or more firm" stay neutral and non-sentimental.
Strongly minimizes disclosure: it explicitly says "you can omit 'dated [date]' unless the date is important" and avoids asking for unnecessary details.
